missing.identify_3_structures_in_the_spotter_t: Identify 3 structures in the Spotter Test
missing.206_bones_support_protection_movement_: 206 bones — support, protection, movement, mineral storage, hematopoiesis.
missing.houses_meninges_brain_and_cranial_nerv: Houses meninges, brain, and cranial nerve foramina. Sutures (coronal, sagittal, lambdoid) fuse by age 2.
missing.600_muscles_movement_posture_heat_prod: 600+ muscles — movement, posture, heat production, joint stabilization.
missing.heart_60_000_miles_of_vessels_5l_of_bl: Heart, 60,000 miles of vessels, 5L of blood — delivers O₂, nutrients, hormones; removes waste.
missing.cns_brain_spinal_cord_and_pns_31_spina: CNS (brain + spinal cord) and PNS (31 spinal nerve pairs, 12 cranial nerve pairs, autonomic NS).
missing.lightest_sleep_transition_from_wakeful: Lightest sleep — transition from wakefulness. Slow rolling eye movements, muscle tone decreasing. Hypnic jerks (myoclonic twitches) common. Easily aroused. Vertex sharp waves appear on EEG.
missing.true_sleep_begins_core_body_temperatur: True sleep begins. Core body temperature drops, heart rate slows. Sleep spindles (thalamocortical bursts) protect sleep and consolidate motor memory. K-complexes are large waveforms responding to external stimuli.
missing.deep_slow_wave_sleep_sws_most_restorat: Deep/slow-wave sleep (SWS). Most restorative stage. Growth hormone secretion peaks. Glymphatic system clears brain waste (amyloid-β). Declarative memory consolidation occurs. Hardest to awaken from; sleep inertia if aroused.
missing.dreaming_stage_brain_is_highly_active_: Dreaming stage. Brain is highly active (similar to waking) but body is paralyzed (atonia via brainstem inhibition of motor neurons). Rapid conjugate eye movements. Emotional memory processing and creative problem-solving. Cycles lengthen through the night.
missing.brodmann_areas_map_52_cytoarchitectura: Brodmann areas map 52 cytoarchitectural regions. Primary motor cortex (area 4), primary somatosensory (areas 3,1,2), primary visual (area 17), primary auditory (areas 41,42). Prefrontal cortex is last to myelinate (age ~25).
missing.purkinje_cells_are_among_the_largest_n: Purkinje cells are among the largest neurons, each receiving ~200,000 synaptic inputs. Cerebellar cortex has 3 layers: molecular, Purkinje, granular. Emerging research shows roles in cognition, emotion, and language.
missing.contains_nuclei_for_cranial_nerves_iii: Contains nuclei for cranial nerves III–XII. Reticular activating system (RAS) is the brain's "on switch" — damage causes coma. Decussation of pyramids in medulla: motor crossover explains contralateral motor control.
missing.memory_replay_during_sleep_hippocampal: Memory replay during sleep: hippocampal neurons "replay" daytime experiences during N3 sleep, transferring memories to neocortex. Grid cells in entorhinal cortex provide spatial coordinates to hippocampal place cells.
missing.fear_conditioning_pathway_auditory_cor: Fear conditioning pathway: auditory cortex → lateral amygdala → central amygdala → hypothalamus (stress response) + PAG (freezing). Amygdala-prefrontal interactions allow emotional regulation — basis of CBT therapy.
missing.key_nuclei_vpl_vpm_somatosensory_relay: Key nuclei: VPL/VPM (somatosensory relay), LGN (visual relay to V1), MGN (auditory relay to A1), pulvinar (attention), anterior nuclear group (memory, Papez circuit).
missing.thermostat_analogy_anterior_hypothalam: Thermostat analogy: anterior hypothalamus triggers cooling (vasodilation, sweating); posterior hypothalamus triggers warming (shivering, vasoconstriction). Fever = elevated set point by prostaglandins from infection.
missing.split_brain_experiments_revealed_hemis: Split-brain experiments revealed hemispheric specialization: left hemisphere = language, logic; right hemisphere = spatial, holistic processing. Information presented to one visual field is processed by contralateral hemisphere.
missing.dopamine_from_substantia_nigra_modulat: Dopamine from substantia nigra modulates the direct (D1, excitatory) and indirect (D2, inhibitory) pathways. Loss of this modulation in Parkinson creates the characteristic difficulty initiating movement.
missing.returns_interstitial_fluid_absorbs_die: Returns interstitial fluid, absorbs dietary fat, immune surveillance — 600–700 lymph nodes, thymus, spleen.
missing.major_visceral_organs_respiration_dige: Major visceral organs — respiration, digestion, filtration, endocrine regulation.
missing.skin_largest_organ_2m_hair_nails_gland: Skin (largest organ, ~2m²), hair, nails, glands — barrier, thermoregulation, sensation, vitamin D synthesis.
missing.oxygen_delivery_and_co_removal_12_20_b: Oxygen delivery and CO₂ removal — ~12–20 breaths/min, ~6L air/min, 300 million alveoli.
missing.hormone_producing_glands_regulate_meta: Hormone-producing glands — regulate metabolism, growth, reproduction, stress, homeostasis via chemical messengers.
missing.male_and_female_reproductive_organs_ga: Male and female reproductive organs — gamete production, fertilization, fetal development, hormonal regulation.
missing.the_circulatory_system_delivers_deoxyg: The circulatory system delivers deoxygenated blood to the lungs, where the respiratory system loads it with oxygen and offloads carbon dioxide across the thin alveolar-capillary membrane.
missing.motor_neurons_from_the_nervous_system_: Motor neurons from the nervous system release acetylcholine at the neuromuscular junction, triggering muscle fiber contraction. Without neural signals, muscles cannot move.
missing.muscles_attach_to_bones_via_tendons_an: Muscles attach to bones via tendons and pull across joints, creating lever systems. The skeleton provides rigid levers; muscles provide the pulling force.
missing.the_hypothalamus_pituitary_axis_releas: The hypothalamus-pituitary axis releases FSH and LH that regulate the gonads. Estrogen, progesterone, and testosterone from reproductive organs feedback to the endocrine system.
missing.the_lymphatic_system_returns_interstit: The lymphatic system returns interstitial fluid to the bloodstream and deploys immune cells made in lymphoid organs. Both systems maintain fluid homeostasis and fight infection.
missing.the_hypothalamus_bridges_the_nervous_a: The hypothalamus bridges the nervous and endocrine systems — it integrates neural signals and translates them into hormonal commands that control the pituitary gland and all downstream hormone cascades.
missing.the_diaphragm_and_intercostal_muscles_: The diaphragm and intercostal muscles physically expand and compress the thoracic cavity to move air. Lungs have no muscle themselves and rely entirely on surrounding muscles.
missing.the_skin_contains_millions_of_speciali: The skin contains millions of specialized nerve endings and encapsulated receptors that detect touch, pressure, temperature, and pain, feeding constant sensory data to the nervous system.
missing.blood_from_the_gi_tract_drains_through: Blood from the GI tract drains through the hepatic portal vein directly to the liver before entering general circulation, allowing the liver to process nutrients and detoxify substances first.
missing.red_bone_marrow_inside_the_skeleton_is: Red bone marrow inside the skeleton is the birthplace of all blood cells. Red blood cells, white blood cells, and platelets are all produced here through hematopoiesis.
missing.which_structure_is_most_likely_affecte: Which structure is most likely affected?
missing.patellofemoral_pain_syndrome_occurs_wh: Patellofemoral pain syndrome occurs when the patella does not track smoothly in its groove on the femur. Repeated stress from running causes cartilage irritation. Treatment includes quad strengthening, hip stabilization, and activity modification.
missing.which_structure_is_most_likely_torn: Which structure is most likely torn?
missing.the_supraspinatus_is_the_most_commonly: The supraspinatus is the most commonly torn rotator cuff muscle. It runs under the acromion where it is susceptible to impingement and tears. Overhead work like painting increases this risk significantly.
missing.which_structure_controls_the_normal_he: Which structure controls the normal heart rhythm?
missing.the_sa_node_in_the_right_atrium_is_the: The SA node in the right atrium is the heart's natural pacemaker. During intense exercise, adrenaline can cause benign palpitations as the heart rate adjusts. Persistent arrhythmias should be evaluated to rule out structural heart disease.
missing.carpal_tunnel_syndrome_is_compression_: Carpal tunnel syndrome is compression of the median nerve under the flexor retinaculum at the wrist. The median nerve supplies sensation to the thumb and first 3.5 fingers. Repetitive wrist use is a major risk factor.
missing.what_diagnosis_must_be_urgently_ruled_: What diagnosis must be urgently ruled out?
missing.painless_lymphadenopathy_with_b_sympto: Painless lymphadenopathy with B-symptoms (night sweats, weight loss, fever) is the classic presentation of Hodgkin lymphoma in young adults. Biopsy showing Reed-Sternberg cells confirms the diagnosis.
missing.which_cells_failed_and_what_is_the_eme: Which cells failed, and what is the emergency condition?
missing.beta_cells_of_islets_of_langerhans_dia: Beta cells of islets of Langerhans; Diabetic Ketoacidosis (DKA)
missing.without_insulin_from_beta_cells_glucos: Without insulin from beta cells, glucose cannot enter cells. The body burns fat, producing ketones that acidify the blood. Kussmaul breathing compensates by exhaling CO2. Treatment: IV fluids, insulin drip, and electrolyte replacement.
missing.why_is_the_middle_third_of_the_clavicl: Why is the middle third of the clavicle the most common fracture site?
missing.the_middle_third_is_thinnest_and_has_n: The middle third is thinnest and has no muscular reinforcement
missing.the_clavicle_is_the_most_frequently_fr: The clavicle is the most frequently fractured bone. Its middle third is thinnest and lacks muscular protection. Force from a fall on an outstretched hand concentrates at this weak point. Most heal with sling immobilization.
missing.why_does_altitude_cause_these_symptoms: Why does altitude cause these symptoms, and which structure is most stressed?
missing.the_alveoli_and_respiratory_muscles_re: The alveoli and respiratory muscles; reduced atmospheric oxygen causes hypoxia
missing.at_high_altitude_atmospheric_pressure_: At high altitude, atmospheric pressure drops, reducing the partial pressure of oxygen. Less oxygen crosses the alveolar membrane. The body compensates by breathing faster and deeper, increasing respiratory muscle work.
missing.follow_a_red_blood_cell_through_the_en: Follow a red blood cell through the entire circulatory system.
missing.deoxygenated_blood_from_the_body_enter: Deoxygenated blood from the body enters the right atrium via the superior and inferior vena cava.
missing.blood_passes_through_the_tricuspid_val: Blood passes through the tricuspid valve into the right ventricle, which pumps it toward the lungs.
missing.the_right_ventricle_ejects_blood_throu: The right ventricle ejects blood through the pulmonary valve into the pulmonary arteries toward both lungs.
missing.in_the_alveolar_capillaries_co2_is_rel: In the alveolar capillaries, CO2 is released and O2 is picked up through the thin alveolar membrane.
missing.freshly_oxygenated_blood_returns_to_th: Freshly oxygenated blood returns to the heart via the four pulmonary veins.
missing.the_four_pulmonary_veins_empty_oxygena: The four pulmonary veins empty oxygenated blood into the left atrium, which fills and then pushes it through the mitral valve.
missing.blood_passes_through_the_mitral_valve_: Blood passes through the mitral valve into the muscular left ventricle, the strongest heart chamber.
missing.the_left_ventricle_powerfully_ejects_b: The left ventricle powerfully ejects blood through the aortic valve into the aorta, the body's main highway.
missing.arteries_branch_into_arterioles_and_ca: Arteries branch into arterioles and capillaries where O2 and nutrients are delivered and CO2/waste collected.
missing.deoxygenated_blood_returns_through_ven: Deoxygenated blood returns through venules and veins, assisted by muscle pumps and valves, back to the right atrium.
missing.trace_the_journey_of_a_breath_of_air_f: Trace the journey of a breath of air from nose to alveoli and back.
missing.air_enters_through_the_nostrils_and_is: Air enters through the nostrils and is warmed, humidified, and filtered by mucous membranes and turbinates.
missing.air_passes_through_the_nasopharynx_and: Air passes through the nasopharynx and oropharynx, a shared passage with the digestive tract.
missing.air_crosses_the_vocal_cords_in_the_voi: Air crosses the vocal cords in the voice box. The epiglottis guards against food entering the airway.
missing.the_trachea_windpipe_is_reinforced_by_: The trachea (windpipe) is reinforced by C-shaped cartilage rings. Cilia move mucus upward to trap debris.
missing.the_trachea_splits_into_left_and_right: The trachea splits into left and right main bronchi, each entering a lung. They branch into smaller bronchioles.
missing.300_million_grape_like_sacs_where_gas_: 300 million grape-like sacs where gas exchange occurs. O2 diffuses into blood; CO2 diffuses out. Surface area equals a tennis court.
missing.the_diaphragm_relaxes_and_rises_reduci: The diaphragm relaxes and rises, reducing lung volume. CO2-rich air is pushed out through the same pathway in reverse.
missing.follow_a_meal_from_mouth_through_the_e: Follow a meal from mouth through the entire digestive system.
missing.teeth_mechanically_break_food_down_sal: Teeth mechanically break food down. Salivary amylase begins starch digestion. The tongue shapes food into a bolus.
missing.swallowing_pushes_the_bolus_past_the_e: Swallowing pushes the bolus past the epiglottis. Peristaltic waves move it down the 25cm esophagus in about 8 seconds.
missing.gastric_acid_ph_1_5_3_5_and_pepsin_bre: Gastric acid (pH 1.5-3.5) and pepsin break down proteins. Churning produces chyme over 2-4 hours.
missing.the_first_25cm_of_the_small_intestine_: The first 25cm of the small intestine receives bile from the liver/gallbladder and enzymes from the pancreas.
missing.most_nutrient_absorption_occurs_here_v: Most nutrient absorption occurs here via villi and microvilli, increasing surface area 600-fold. 6-7 meters long.
missing.water_and_electrolytes_are_reabsorbed_: Water and electrolytes are reabsorbed. Gut bacteria ferment remaining fiber, producing vitamins K and B12.
missing.waste_is_compacted_and_stored_in_the_r: Waste is compacted and stored in the rectum until defecation. The entire journey takes 24-72 hours.
missing.trace_a_reflex_arc_from_stimulus_to_re: Trace a reflex arc from stimulus to response.
missing.a_sensory_receptor_in_the_skin_detects: A sensory receptor in the skin detects a painful stimulus (like touching a hot surface) and generates an electrical signal.
missing.the_signal_travels_along_a_sensory_aff: The signal travels along a sensory (afferent) neuron toward the spinal cord at up to 120 m/s via saltatory conduction.
missing.in_the_dorsal_horn_the_sensory_neuron_: In the dorsal horn, the sensory neuron synapses with an interneuron. For reflexes, the signal does not need to reach the brain.
missing.the_interneuron_in_the_spinal_gray_mat: The interneuron in the spinal gray matter integrates the signal and relays it to a motor neuron.
missing.the_motor_efferent_neuron_carries_the_: The motor (efferent) neuron carries the command signal from the ventral horn down to the target muscle.
missing.acetylcholine_released_at_the_neuromus: Acetylcholine released at the neuromuscular junction causes muscle contraction. You pull your hand away before feeling pain.
missing.meanwhile_a_copy_of_the_signal_ascends: Meanwhile, a copy of the signal ascends to the somatosensory cortex. You consciously feel pain about 0.5 seconds after the reflex.
missing.sorry_i_could_not_connect_to_the_ai_tu: Sorry, I could not connect to the AI tutor right now.
missing.ai_tutor_is_not_available_in_this_envi: AI tutor is not available in this environment.
missing.click_any_organ_to_see_its_structure_f: Click any organ to see its structure, function, and connections. Hover for plain-English labels; tap for the deep dive.
missing.step_through_each_major_part_in_pedago: Step through each major part in pedagogical order. Best path for first-time learners — covers structure → function → integration in one pass.
missing.no_system_works_alone_the_kidney_filte: No system works alone. The kidney filters blood (cardiovascular), responds to ADH (endocrine), and excretes urea (digestive byproduct). Find the links.
missing.ask_a_question_about_the_active_organ_: Ask a question about the active organ or system. The tutor knows what is on screen and tailors answers to your grade band.
missing.click_the_named_structure_on_the_diagr: Click the named structure on the diagram. Builds the click-the-thing reflex that AP and medical exams rely on.
missing.follow_blood_air_food_or_signals_throu: Follow blood, air, food, or signals through the body in sequence. Pathways are how isolated facts become a system you can reason about.
missing.quick_recall_cards_with_spaced_repetit: Quick-recall cards with spaced repetition. Six-second review now beats five-minute re-read tomorrow — that is the testing effect.
missing.adjust_the_sliders_to_keep_each_body_v: Adjust the sliders to keep each body variable in its healthy range, and watch how the systems work together to maintain balance.
missing.reset_all_layers_to_default_skin_only: Reset all layers to default (skin only)
missing.switch_between_male_and_female_reprodu: Switch between male and female reproductive anatomy
missing.exception_the_pulmonary_artery_carries: Exception: the pulmonary artery carries deoxygenated blood (blue), the pulmonary veins carry oxygenated blood (red).
missing.which_body_system_contains_this_struct: Which body system contains this structure?
missing.ask_a_question_about_anatomy_to_get_st: Ask a question about anatomy to get started!
missing.ask_the_anatomy_ai_tutor_a_question: Ask the anatomy AI tutor a question
missing.no_tour_available_for_this_system: No tour available for this system.
missing.a_pin_is_placed_on_the_anatomical_figu: A pin is placed on the anatomical figure. Identify the structure as quickly as you can! Look for the pulsing crosshair on the canvas.
missing.what_structure_is_marked_on_the_figure: What structure is marked on the figure?
missing.trace_step_by_step_how_blood_flows_air: Trace step-by-step how blood flows, air moves, food digests, or nerve signals travel through the body.
missing.no_flashcards_available_for_this_compl: No flashcards available for this complexity level.
missing.all_vital_signs_within_physiologic_ran: All vital signs within physiologic range.
missing.compensatory_mechanisms_activated_body: Compensatory mechanisms activated. Body restoring set points.
missing.multiple_systems_strained_risk_of_deco: Multiple systems strained. Risk of decompensation.
missing.acid_base_glucose_thermal_disturbance_: Acid-base + glucose + thermal disturbance overwhelms compensation.
missing.hypothesis_free_text_which_vital_sign_: Hypothesis (free text): Which vital sign tolerates the least deviation?
missing.hold_two_vital_signs_steady_move_the_t: Hold two vital signs steady. Move the third. Watch.
missing.real_human_ph_range_is_7_35_7_45_inves: Real human pH range is 7.35-7.45. Investigate why so tight.
missing.find_two_settings_producing_critical_s: Find two settings producing critical state. What do they share?
missing.explain_why_homeostasis_is_fragile_to_: Explain why homeostasis is fragile to multi-variable deviation.
missing.design_note_discrete_4_state_vital_mar: Design note: discrete 4-state vital marker; no clinical score; no reveal — by design.
